entry.title: Confirmation of Scope Item 5XU activation and Basic Statutory Reporting
entry.content:
1)The Tax Procedure 0TXIN exists in our Quality System. Does this indicate that Scope Item 5XU is fully active?

2. Availability of Basic Statutory Reports
The "Basic Compliance Reporting" functionalities included in the standard license. is it possible to generate the following minimum required tax reports using the "Run Statutory Reports" (App ID: F1515) app?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Confirmation of Scope Item 5XU activation and Basic S...
entry.content:
Dear all,

 

Please find the below answer,

In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, no GST components are maintained under 0TXIN. GST works under SAP-delivered India tax configuration, not the legacy 0TXIN procedure.

Under India GST in Public Cloud, the following components are delivered within procedure 0TXIN. Example below,

GST Tax Components (Condition Types inside 0TXIN)

SAP delivers GST-relevant condition types such as:

CGST – Central GST

SGST – State GST

IGST – Integrated GST

UTGST – Union Territory GST

These are embedded in 0TXIN and activated via tax codes.

Yes, it is possible to generate the following minimum required tax reports using the run statutory reports app. The Document and Reporting Compliance (5XU) scope item fulfills all real-time and statutory reporting requirements, including end-to-end processing of transaction controls, digital link, and online submission requirements. Enabling visibility of advanced features of SAP Document and Reporting Compliance.

 

Best regards,

Atanu

SAP Support - S/4HANA Cloud

entry.title: Hi experts, I have a below scenario about posting unplanned scrap quantity in header level of produ
entry.content:
Hi experts,

 

I have a below scenario about posting unplanned scrap quantity in header level of production order.

 

If we have a production order for 10 pcs and while producing the assembly (GR is not posted, but only GI is posted) before the final operation is performed the assembly is found to be defective and it has to be scrapped. So we do a partial confirmation for with scrap quantity as 1 pc and this scrap quantity gets posted in operations level of production order .

 

But in production order header still the order quantity will be 10 pcs and delivered quantity is 0. Scrap portion and shortage deviation is 0.

 

Now MRP considers that still order has 10 pcs of order quantity, but in reality the MES system has only 9 more pieced to be produced and this will prevent MRP in creating new planned orders .

We always do partial confirmation for each parts and in case of any repair or rework or scrapping the production takes in months to take a decision and this is causing issues in planning

I am thinking if we can use BAPI_PRODORD_CHANGE by passing the scrap quantity in order header level, by doing so my order quantity will be 9 pcs and it will generate the dependent requirements accordingly.

 

I would like to know if anyone else has faced this issue and can we update the unplanned scrap quantity in production order header level?

entry.title: Re: Improving the SAP Community together, one comment at a time
entry.content:
Thank you for your long comment @Marian_Zeis .

Out of curiosity, do you have any concrete suggestions on what we should be doing? Us as in everyone in the community.
Why do you feel it is no longer the community's/everyone's job to educate and guide?
And what do you mean by "SAP gives the moderators muskets" -> muskets?
We, the community team/moderators/content reviewers, are also part of the community, and we will continue to educate, remove content that should be removed and ensure that the community platform is an inclusive place where everyone feels welcome. Nothing will change from our side, we will continue to moderate, manage and curate as we have all along. 

Once a user has publishing rights, anyone can publish content without going through moderation review. It would be an impossible task if the Community team should read every single post published in the community every day. And even if we were to stop all AI-generated content, where should we draw a line that can be clearly communicated? 60% AI-generated? 38% AI-generated? And by using which detector? (the AI detectors don't even agree among themselves...)

We have clear AI-guidelines, that obviously not all users are adhering to. 
If you take AI out of the equation, the problem we are seeing is that the "AI-slop" posts are low quality posts. 
These "AI slop" posts are not all factually incorrect, they don't contain misleading information, most of the ABAP code we have checked would run although it is  often old and could/should be improved - and from our side, we can no longer undeniably prove in our conversations with the authors, that they have used AI to write the posts. 

What is missing? Which hurdles should be removed?
What specifically is it you feel is missing now that was there before the last platform migration? 
I read all the feedback, but what I miss is more concrete actionable feedback. For all of us.

And just so you know, you can write to us to let us know you don't agree with a moderation decision and briefly explain why. Content review on the platform is done by human beings, and we do our best to make nuanced decisions. We sometimes make mistakes as well 🙂

Where do you think Joule Agents will create the biggest impact in Utilities?

  1. Billing exceptions and meter data validation
  2. Credit & collections automation
  3. Field service scheduling and dispatch
  4. Outage and customer communication
  5. Clean Core extensions for IS‑U
  6.  Something else entirely

Would love to hear real-world perspectives from consultants, architects, and customers. This is one of the biggest shifts coming to Utilities in 2026, and community insights will help all of us prepare.

entry.title: Re: Can BAPI_MTRREADDOC_UPLOAD create specific meter reading orders depending on registers?
entry.content: Yes — you can use BAPI_MTRREADDOC_UPLOAD to create meter reading documents for specific registers, but you cannot create meter reading orders with it. You can only upload results for registers that already exist in the installation facts.

entry.title: Re: Creation Large amount of Meter Reading Orders via ISU_S_METERREAD_CREATE | MSG EL422
entry.content:
You’re not hitting a functional error — you’re hitting debugger and batch‑processing limitations. The EL422 message is triggered indirectly by the mass‑processing framework, not because you’re actually using IDocs or the MR_UPLOAD BAPI.

The real issue is:

  • 100k installations in one LUW

  • Debugger timeout / memory exhaustion

  • MR creation framework raising EL422 internally

  • Your session being terminated before you can see the next message

entry.title: Re: Deep Dive into SAP Datasphere Object Store of BDC: Benefits, Architecture and implementation
entry.content:
@albertosimeoni thanks for sharing your experiences, it's very insightful, and (unfortunately) painful to read...

It is really "problematic" if the consumption of compute and API calls is actually contributing that much, never would have thought of it to become that significant factor with the Object Store.

I guess that it can still be effectively used for "cold storage", with only occasional writes (appends) and reads from time to time, but that was not the original value proposal!

Given that data products have to be persisted in the Object Store, and that all these new Intelligent Applications are built on data products - clearly "the push" is to use it similar to what you were doing; but then the question is how is this new "standardized offering" (Intelligent Apps & co.) going to perform, and how much is it going to cost...

Btw. really nice point with the mandated minimum of 7 days of keeping previous versions and it's 7-fold impact on storage consumption, although in the opposite direction - it is true that the Object Store can store data say ~2x more efficiently...

Anyways, good luck! 🙂

entry.title: Re: SAP HANA Rebates: Partner determination for Contract...
entry.content:
Hi @mirthalalozanocorrea, 

I’m not completely sure I understand your requirement, so let me clarify a few points.

First, in SAP Settlement Management (Condition Contracts), the contract is created with a Customer Business Partner (rebate recipient). It is not based on the “Invoicing Party” as a vendor unless you are in a supplier rebate scenario.

If your question is whether you can base the rebate calculation on Ship-To Party instead of Bill-To (Payer/Invoicing Party), then yes, this is possible.

There are two separate aspects to consider:

  1. Business volume selection
    Yes, you can base the rebate on Ship-To Party.
    In the Business Volume Selection Criteria, there is a standard field combination that includes Ship-To (KUNWE) — for example field combination 0032.
    The field KUNWE (Ship-To) is part of the standard CDS view VWB2_VBRKVBRP_1, which is used by SAP CCM to determine business volume. Therefore, the system can select invoices based on Ship-To.

    So even if the contract owner is Customer A, you can configure the contract to consider all billing documents where Ship-To = 1234, for example.

  2. Settlement Document Partner 
    The Settlement Document itself will always require a Bill-To partner.

In standard:

  • The customer owner of the contract becomes the Bill-To in the settlement document.

  • A settlement document cannot be created if the BP does not have the required partner functions (e.g. Bill-To).


Best Regards, 
Gauthier VICTOR

entry.title: Issue with AI160 JavaScript Exercise – /api/agent/trigger-agent returning HTML instead of JSON
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I’m currently working through the excellent TechEd 2025 AI160 – “Build Your Own AI Agent-Based Solution with the Generative AI Hub” hands-on, specifically the JavaScript exercise.

My setup:

  • Productive SAP AI Core instance

  • Provisioned in SAP BTP Cockpit (extended plan)

  • Service keys fully configured

  • Running the tutorial using npm run tutorial in SAP Business Application Studio

The UI (port 3002), Agent (port 3001), and Mock Server (port 3000) all start successfully.

However, when triggering the agent from the UI, I get the following error in the browser:

Error: Failed to execute 'json' on 'Response':
Unexpected token '<', "<!DOCTYPE "... is not valid JSON

 In the Network tab, I see:

GET /api/agent/trigger-agent → 404 / HTML response

I verified:

  • Vite proxy is configured to forward /api → http://localhost:3001

  • Agent server is running on port 3001

  • Endpoint /api/agent/trigger-agent exists in server.ts

  • x-thread-id header is being sent from the UI

Despite this, the response is sometimes HTML (404 / error page) instead of JSON, causing the parsing error.

Has anyone faced a similar issue with AI160 JavaScript exercises?
Is there anything specific to check regarding routing, proxying, or error handling when using productive AI Core instances?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated. @ZhongpinWang, @deekshasinha

Thank you!

entry.title: Re: Sending Email notifications for Time Valuation Alerts
entry.content:
Hi @NeeleshKamath NeeleshKamath,

Thanks for this blog.  I have known that we can use MDF for notification and for sure it can work well.

However the main challenge is that the effective date is NULL for MDF with Effective Dating as NONE (which already mentioned in this KBA 2870640 - Why is the Effective Date column in Manage Workflow Requests blank. So the notification email is confused for employees because the date is missing. Unless HR need to go to timesheet and check the Alert in TS like below. You know users will NOT accept this.

I think the solution given by SAP does not fully cover the common business processes. In reality emp can adjust and update working time backdated, therefore notification email for who (less say working more than 10 hrs a day) to manager and HR should clearly indicate the date that this event occurs.

Work around solution  WAS:

We can configure 366 different WF with document templates and 'hardcode' the date and month (not year) in the WF document template and then check date in MDF business rule and call the corresponding WF. 

We must create 366 different document template too.

But this WAS is you know not good at all.

Hope we have better approach now.

Best regards,

Woody Nguyen

entry.title: Re: Sending Email notifications for Time Valuation Alerts
entry.content:
Hi @GaneshBoina 

There are some solutions to do that.

Option 1: not allow emp to submit time sheet if they do NOT key in attendance or absence for all workdays (according to work schedule). Then it is now responsibility that emp must input.

And the missing Time sheet will result deduction in payroll side, then emp will do by themselves for sure. 

This one I have implemented for many clients and so far working OK.

Option 2: You can rely on Time Valuation Alert as mentioned above and sync records from Time Valuation Alert to your MDF via IC and filter in IC is today - 5 days. Now having all data in your MDF then you can trigger notification as normal.

Option 3: You can check where Time Recording is updated -> it could be Time Recording MDF or Employee Time MDF. Then similar sync records to your MDF and send notification from your own MDF.

The key for Option 2 and Option 3 that you must know how to use Function Lookup in SFEC (not SFEC TT). This func lookup is the base function and as consultant must learn how to use.

You can let try to sync your MDF daily via IC (easily to do that with IC) and then from this MDF use lookup function to read data from Time Valuation Alert mdf for Time Recording mdf or Employee Time mdf.

If u read data from Time Valuation Alert mdf -> then logic you must build in Time Valuation TV and make sure it covers that only generates alerts via TV if missing both Attendance and Absence. If Absence is there then should NOT generate alert via TV.

For Time Recording mdf or Employee Time mdf, you must read by yourself to ensure ONLY send notification from your MDF if both Attendance and Absence are missing.

Please note the limitation with MDF if Effective Dating is NONE in configuration object, then effective date is empty. 

Hope it is useful for you. 

Best regards,

Woody Nguyen

entry.title: Re: Trying reconcile CO09 with BAPI_MATERIAL_AVAILABILITY
entry.content:
Thank you, Anil.  But I’m not following the math.

Let’s focus on the first point.

You suggest:

Look at the lines

  • MRP element = BtsLoc
  • Stock segment = MIXTEST1 / FG
  • On the requested date (02/13/2026)
  • Check the “Cumulative. ATP” column

 You will see 880 as the cumulative available quantity. This is calculated as:

  • Total stock (973)
  • Minus open requirements (132 – 60 = 72)
  • Resulting ATP = 880

 That 880 is exactly what the BAPI returns in COM_QTY.

Where is that? I see Cumulative ATP as 972 on row BtSloc.

What combination of 973,132, 60 & 72 results in 880?

I appreciate your assistance.

Leo

entry.title: The Procurement Monthly - Feb 2026
entry.content:
Summary. 1

Closing the procurement value gap – World CC. 1

The 2026 ProcureCon CPO report. 2

Gartner - Leadership vision 2026 – CPO priorities. 2

Procurement application predictions for 2026 – IDC. 2

2026 supply chain outlook – S&P Global Market Intelligence. 3

Proxima 2026 CPO report. 3

Agentic AI in procurement – Kearney. 3

Interos 2026 predictions – key trends for procurement. 4

 

Summary

Reading these reports, to me I think that Procurement is impacting and being impacted by three forces in 2026: AI moving from pilots to production, supply risk growing more complex, and contract value quietly leaking out of the business.

Across the research, AI is now a default expectation, not a differentiator, but few organizations feel fully ready to use it at scale.

Contract value leakage averages around 11 percent of spend, supply chains face persistent tariff and trade volatility, and AI costs and risks are starting to bite. With all of this, I see three constant themes emerging:

  • Treat contracting and post‑award governance as a core commercial capability, not an administrative step.
  • Redesign roles so humans and AI agents work as a single operating model, especially in sourcing, contract management, and supplier risk.
  • Shift procurement performance from “savings only” to a balanced view of risk, resilience, growth, and realized contract value.

Closing the procurement value gap – World CC 
World CC finds organizations lose on average 11 percent of contract value after signature through missed savings, unmanaged price changes, weak handovers, and dormant innovation clauses. A business with 500 million in contracted spend can easily leak 15–75 million each year. The core issue is structural: procurement and legal focus on pre‑award work, then exit just as value delivery starts. Roles, governance, and data do not support continuous contract management, and standard templates are too rigid for volatile markets.

The procurement function can reduce leakage by redesigning the operating model so accountability for outcomes is explicit, adopting adaptive contract terms (indexed pricing, clear change controls, structured renewal triggers), and using AI‑enabled CLM to track obligations, price adjustments, and performance at scale. This needs investment in post‑award capability, supplier relationship management, and a culture where contracts are treated as live commercial instruments, not static PDFs.

The 2026 ProcureCon CPO report
According to the report, CPO influence continues to grow: 89 percent of respondents say their CPO plays more of a role in high‑level decisions than in prior years, and 82 percent expect that influence to increase further. Yet only 11 percent see their CPO as a core executive peer today.

Top strategic priorities for 2026 are strengthening supplier partnerships (55 percent), implementing AI‑driven automation (45 percent), and supporting growth through strategic sourcing (42 percent), while key challenges include securing digital talent (54 percent), balancing cost and growth (52 percent), and rising sustainability demands (46 percent).

AI is funded mostly as part of broader digital programs, with only 11 percent of organizations claiming to be fully ready and already seeing measurable AI impact. Barriers include data privacy and compliance concerns, poor data quality and integration, and cultural resistance to AI. For the procurement function, this points to four moves: build a talent pipeline with analytics skills, fix data foundations before scaling AI, invest in integrated cloud platforms and supplier risk tools, and develop board‑ready storytelling that connects procurement outcomes to growth, risk, and ESG.

Gartner - Leadership vision 2026 – CPO priorities
Research for 2026 highlights three pressures on CPOs: productivity expectations, complex supplier risk, and rising AI costs. AI usage has grown, yet productivity gains remain trapped because roles have not been redesigned; teams still bolt AI onto old job designs. CPOs are urged to create roles that treat some tasks as “AI‑native,” centralize risk intelligence so experts act on a filtered view rather than raw feeds, and put discipline around AI commercial models so usage‑based fees do not outstrip value.

For the procurement function, that means moving from isolated AI pilots to a coordinated operating model where agents handle monitoring, drafting, and first‑level analysis, while humans decide, negotiate, and manage relationships. It also means treating AI spend like any other major category: clear unit economics, demand governance, and regular reviews of model usage and value delivered.

Procurement application predictions for 2026 – IDC
2026 is set to be a “show me” year for procurement AI. After broad experimentation in 2025, CFOs now want clear ROI and predictable pricing, and will resist AI offers that create volatile monthly costs. On the application side, several trends stand out:

  • Headless and modular procurement apps are emerging, with API‑first designs that plug into wider business workflows.
  • Source‑to‑pay suites are pushing back with broader coverage and AI‑infused features, while contract lifecycle management remains an active area for analytics and obligation management.
  • Sourcing and direct spend get renewed focus, with AI‑supported scenarios, market intelligence, and multi‑tier visibility.
  • Vendors are testing API and token‑based charging models, which raises budgeting complexity for procurement.

The technology roadmap for procurement should stress integration readiness, strong APIs, and clarity on AI features that genuinely improve sourcing quality, supplier risk decisions, or contract performance, rather than adding another dashboard. Pricing structures for AI features need to be negotiated upfront with clear guardrails.

2026 supply chain outlook – S&P Global Market Intelligence
Supply chain leaders face persistent tariff volatility alongside a more supportive trade environment. Firms absorbed an estimated 907 billion in tariff‑related costs in 2025, with many planning successive price increases into 2026 and attempts to share costs with suppliers. Trade deals across Europe, Asia, and Latin America are expected to support real export growth over the next five years, yet US sectoral duties and export controls, including those affecting AI‑enabling technologies, still create uncertainty.

Reshoring remains a focus, though it no longer guarantees growth for emerging markets as automation reduces the simple advantage of low labour cost. For procurement, this means category strategies must account for shifting tariff regimes, the trade footprint of AI supply chains, and the fact that supplier financial resilience directly affects margins when shocks hit.

Across the report there are consistent themes of persistent global disruptions, the importance of financially healthy suppliers, and the need for better risk sensing across supply tiers. Digitalization of supply chain visibility is advancing, but many teams are still stuck in reactive mode, drowning in risk signals without synthesized views.

Procurement can respond by tightening collaboration with supply chain and risk functions around shared metrics on resilience, on‑time performance, and supplier health, and by investing in tools that integrate external risk data with contract and spend data. This supports faster decisions on reallocation of demand, contract renegotiation, or dual‑sourcing.

Proxima 2026 CPO report
Proxima’s 2026 CPO research shows AI at the top of every CPO conversation, with most organizations rolling out a small number of defined use cases and productivity guardrails rather than chasing full automation. Resilience and operating model redesign follow closely: CPOs are seeking board support to justify paying a premium for continuity of supply, and many are reframing procurement’s primary metric from cost to risk.

A key insight is that AI is no longer seen as a differentiator on its own; it is expected. Advantage will come from making deliberate choices about where AI solves real problems end‑to‑end, for example linking demand forecasting, should‑cost, negotiations, and supplier monitoring in one flow. Underpinning all of this is leadership: CPOs are being asked to provide commercial judgement on risk, guide AI adoption, and act as connectors across functions.

Agentic AI in procurement – Kearney

Kearney describes agentic AI as the shift from tools that support people to digital workforces that can decide and act across the procurement cycle. Confusion is common, with many leaders still equating it with generative AI or simple automation. Early adopters report significant cost reductions in some processes and much faster decision cycles when agents handle tasks such as tail spend negotiations, supplier qualification, or risk monitoring.

For the procurement function, the near‑term opportunity lies in targeted use: agents that orchestrate sourcing events, monitor contract obligations, or run “always on” tail spend sourcing, while humans handle strategy, exceptions, and supplier relationships. This model depends on comprehensive, well‑governed data and strong oversight so agents act within clear commercial and ethical boundaries.

Interos 2026 predictions – key trends for procurement
Interos positions supply chains as the front line of a new economic battlefield in 2026, shaped by converging geopolitical, technological, financial, and labour shocks. For the procurement function, the report signals a shift from managing isolated supplier incidents to managing systemic, multi‑country, multi‑tier disruption as standard operating conditions.

Key points from the report include

  1. Supply chains as geopolitical targets
    State conflict and sanctions are expected to focus more directly on supply routes, logistics hubs, and key suppliers, including in technology and critical industries. Procurement must treat geopolitical exposure as a core sourcing variable, not a side consideration.
  2. Data weaponization and AI sovereignty
    Governments and blocs are tightening rules on where data lives and how AI is trained, creating “AI sovereignty” zones. Data flows, cloud providers, and AI infrastructure choices now carry political and compliance risk that procurement needs to assess when awarding contracts.
  3. Rare earth and critical mineral constraints
    Constraints on rare earths and other strategic materials will create bottlenecks across electronics, automotive, and clean energy supply chains. Procurement will need deeper visibility into upstream mining, refining, and geopolitical exposure, along with contingency sourcing strategies.
  4. AI data centre bottlenecks
    AI infrastructure is capacity‑constrained, from chips to power and cooling. Suppliers that depend on cloud and AI services may face performance or cost shocks. Procurement should treat data centre location, power security, and cloud concentration as core risk criteria in major deals.

As always reach out to discuss more, and always happy to hear your thoughts!

entry.title: Re: The JWT token only contains the openid scope, but non...
entry.content:
I was able to resolve issue. It might be specific to my requirement, where attributes are not mandatory. 

Template worked for me: 

{
"xsappname": "my-app-reprocess-v2",
"tenant-mode": "dedicated",
"description": "Message Reprocessing Application - Security Configuration",
"scopes": [
{
"name": "$XSAPPNAME.ReprocessViewer",
"description": "View messages, content, and statistics (read-only access)"
},
{
"name": "$XSAPPNAME.ReprocessAdministrator",
"description": "Full administrative access including reprocess operations and CRUD"
}
],
"role-templates": [
{
"name": "ReprocessViewer",
"description": "Read-Only Access - View messages and statistics",
"scope-references": [
"$XSAPPNAME.ReprocessViewer"
]
},
{
"name": "ReprocessAdministrator",
"description": "Full Administrative Access - CRUD operations and reprocess actions",
"scope-references": [
"$XSAPPNAME.ReprocessAdministrator",
"$XSAPPNAME.ReprocessViewer"
]
}
],
"role-collections": [
{
"name": "MessageReprocessViewersRC",
"description": "Message Reprocess Viewers - Read-only access",
"role-template-references": [
"$XSAPPNAME.ReprocessViewer"
]
},
{
"name": "MessageReprocessAdministratorsRC",
"description": "Message Reprocess Administrators - Full access",
"role-template-references": [
"$XSAPPNAME.ReprocessAdministrator"
]
}
],
"authorities": [
"$ACCEPT_GRANTED_AUTHORITIES"
],
"oauth2-configuration": {
"grant-types": [
"authorization_code",
"client_credentials",
"refresh_token"
],
"credential-types": ["binding-secret", "x509"],
"redirect-uris": [
"https://*.cfapps.example.com/**",
],
"token-validity": 3600,
"refresh-token-validity": 86400,
"autoapprove": true
}
}

Key differences are in table below:

Key Differences:  
   

Feature

Configuration A (With Attributes)Configuration B (Working)
attributes section✅ Defines email attribute❌ Not present
attribute-references in role-templates✅ Both roles reference email❌ Not present
authorities array❌ Not present✅ ["$ACCEPT_GRANTED_AUTHORITIES"]
oauth2-configuration.grant-types❌ Not specified (uses defaults)✅ Explicit: authorization_code, client_credentials, refresh_token
oauth2-configuration.autoapprove❌ Not specified✅ true

entry.title: Re: Cannot deploy CAP application in SAP BTP Trial – stuc...
entry.content: Thank you for your suggestion. Even when I run mvn cds:watch from the srv folder, the project root still needs to be detected correctly by the cds-maven-plugin. In my case, I had .cdsrc.json both in the project root and under srv/, so the plugin picked srv/ as the working directory and tried to access srv/srv. After moving the production configuration to the root .cdsrc.json and removing srv/.cdsrc.json, mvn cds:watch works correctly now. Thank you very much for your help.

entry.title: SAP BTP XSUAA Security Configuration Comparison: Attribute-Based vs. Authority-Based Approaches
entry.content:
In SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) Cloud Foundry applications, configuring XSUAA (Extended Services for User Account and Authentication) via the xs-security.json file is a critical step for secure authentication and authorization.

Sample Application used for this article - "Message Reprocessing Application". This scenario try to cover 2 roles - "ReprocessViewer" and "ReprocessAdministrator".

Two main approaches exist:

 

  • Configuration A: Explicit attribute-based – manual mapping and control
  • Configuration B: Authority-based – automatic acceptance of IDP attributes (recommended for most cases)

 

Understanding the differences helps you balance security, compliance, simplicity, and development speed.

Quick Comparison Table

Feature                          | Config A (Explicit Attributes)          | Config B (Authority-Based)
---------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------
attributes section               | ✅ Present (defines email, etc.)        | ❌ Not needed
attribute-references in roles    | ✅ Yes (links attributes to roles)      | ❌ Not present
authorities array                | ❌ Not used                              | ✅ "$ACCEPT_GRANTED_AUTHORITIES"
oauth2 grant-types               | ❌ Uses defaults                         | ✅ Explicit list (authorization_code + others)
oauth2 autoapprove               | ❌ Not set (shows consent screen)       | ✅ true (smooth internal login)

Key takeaway from the table

Configuration A: Explicit Attribute-Based Approach

Structure (xs-security.json excerpt):

JSON

{
  "xsappname": "my-app-reprocess-v2",
  "tenant-mode": "dedicated",
  "description": "Message Reprocessing Application - Security Configuration",
  "scopes": [
    {
      "name": "$XSAPPNAME.ReprocessViewer",
      "description": "View messages, content, and statistics (read-only access)"
    },
    {
      "name": "$XSAPPNAME.ReprocessAdministrator",
      "description": "Full administrative access including reprocess operations and CRUD"
    }
  ],
  "attributes": [
    {
      "name": "email",
      "description": "User email address",
      "valueType": "string"
    }
  ],
  "role-templates": [
    {
      "name": "ReprocessViewer",
      "description": "Read-Only Access - View messages and statistics",
      "scope-references": ["$XSAPPNAME.ReprocessViewer"],
      "attribute-references": ["email"]
    },
    {
      "name": "ReprocessAdministrator",
      "description": "Full Administrative Access - CRUD operations and reprocess actions",
      "scope-references": [
        "$XSAPPNAME.ReprocessAdministrator",
        "$XSAPPNAME.ReprocessViewer"
      ],
      "attribute-references": ["email"]
    }
  ],
  "role-collections": [
    {
      "name": "MessageReprocessViewersRC",
      "description": "Message Reprocess Viewers - Read-only access",
      "role-template-references": ["$XSAPPNAME.ReprocessViewer"]
    },
    {
      "name": "MessageReprocessAdministratorsRC",
      "description": "Message Reprocess Administrators - Full access",
      "role-template-references": ["$XSAPPNAME.ReprocessAdministrator"]
    }
  ],
  "oauth2-configuration": {
    "credential-types": ["binding-secret", "x509"],
    "redirect-uris": ["https://*.cfapps.example.com/**", "http://localhost:*/**"],
    "token-validity": 3600,
    "refresh-token-validity": 86400
  }
}

Advantages

 

  • Explicit control over exposed attributes
  • Fine-grained security and compliance
  • Enables attribute transformation and ABAC

 

Disadvantages

 

  • Higher maintenance
  • More complex configuration
  • Less flexible with IDP changes

 

When to use: Regulated industries (healthcare, finance, government), ABAC needs, multi-tenant SaaS, strict governance.

Configuration B: Authority-Based Approach (Recommended)

Structure (xs-security.json excerpt):

JSON

{
  "xsappname": "my-app-reprocess-v2",
  "tenant-mode": "dedicated",
  "description": "Message Reprocessing Application - Security Configuration",
  "scopes": [ /* same as above */ ],
  "role-templates": [ /* same as above, without attribute-references */ ],
  "role-collections": [ /* same as above */ ],
  "authorities": ["$ACCEPT_GRANTED_AUTHORITIES"],
  "oauth2-configuration": {
    "grant-types": ["authorization_code", "client_credentials", "refresh_token"],
    "credential-types": ["binding-secret", "x509"],
    "redirect-uris": ["https://*.cfapps.example.com/**", "http://localhost:*/**"],
    "token-validity": 3600,
    "refresh-token-validity": 86400,
    "autoapprove": true
  }
}

Advantages

 

  • Simplicity and minimal config
  • Automatic handling of standard IDP attributes
  • Lower maintenance, faster development
  • Seamless with SAP IAS, Azure AD, Okta

 

Disadvantages

 

  • Less granular control (all attributes passed)
  • Potential over-exposure

 

When to use (most cases): Standard enterprise apps, RBAC, rapid development, microservices, agile environments.

Deep Dive: Key Differences

 

  • Attributes & attribute-references — Explicit in A, automatic in B via $ACCEPT_GRANTED_AUTHORITIES
  • Authorities — Special directive in B accepts all granted attributes/scopes from trusted IDP
  • OAuth grant-types & autoapprove — Explicit in B for predictability and better UX (no consent screen for internal apps)

 

JWT Token Examples

Configuration A (Explicit):

JSON

{
  "user_name": "john.doe",
  "email": "john.doe@example.com",
  "scope": ["my-app-reprocess-v2.ReprocessViewer"],
  "xs.user.attributes": { "email": ["john.doe@example.com"] }
}

Configuration B (Automatic):

JSON

{
  "user_name": "john.doe",
  "email": "john.doe@example.com",
  "given_name": "John",
  "family_name": "Doe",
  "scope": ["my-app-reprocess-v2.ReprocessViewer"],
  "xs.user.attributes": { "email": ["john.doe@example.com"], "department": ["Engineering"], "cost_center": ["CC-1234"] }
}

Application Code Impact (Examples)

SAP CAP (Node.js) – Configuration B:

JavaScript

this.before('READ', 'Messages', async (req) => {
  const userEmail = req.user.attr.email;
  const department = req.user.attr.department;  // extra attributes available
  // ...
});

Spring Boot (Java) – Configuration B:

Java

String email = token.getAttributeFromClaimAsString("email");
String department = token.getAttributeFromClaimAsString("department");

Migration Guide & Troubleshooting

(Refer to the detailed steps in original content for From A → B and vice versa, plus common issues like missing attributes, invalid grant types, consent screens, token size.)

Security & Performance Best Practices

 

  • Principle of least privilege
  • Validate JWT properly (@sap/xssec)
  • Specific redirect URIs (avoid wildcards in prod)
  • Appropriate token lifetimes
  • Prefer X.509 in production
  • Monitor token size (switch to A if >4KB)

 

Recommendations by Use Case

Use CaseRecommendedRationaleInternal Enterprise AppConfiguration BSimplicity & standard integrationRegulated IndustryConfiguration AExplicit governance & audit trailRapid Prototype/MVPConfiguration BMinimal configAttribute-Based AuthorizationConfiguration ARequired for ABAC

Conclusion For most SAP BTP applications in 2025, go with Configuration B (Authority-Based) — it delivers simplicity, flexibility, and aligns with modern OAuth/OIDC patterns while reducing maintenance.

Only choose Configuration A when you need strict attribute control, custom transformations, or operate in highly regulated environments.

What approach are you using in your SAP BTP projects? Have you migrated between the two? Share your experiences or questions in the comments — happy to discuss architecture or troubleshooting!

#SAPBTP #XSUAA #CloudFoundry #SAPSecurity #Authorization #IdentityManagement #SAPDeveloper

entry.title: Re: SAP BTP XSUAA Security Configuration Comparison: Attribute-Based vs. Authority-Based Approaches
entry.content: It took me quite a while to figure this out. Hope this saves you from the same headaches and trial-and-error hoops I went through!

entry.title: Re: Trying reconcile CO09 with BAPI_MATERIAL_AVAILABILITY
entry.content:
Hi Leo,

Great question — and thank you for sharing the screenshot. The reason you’re seeing 972 instead of 880 is because the CO09 line you’re looking at is not the batch‑level ATP that the BAPI uses.

In CO09, the Cumulative ATP value depends on which row you select:

  1. What the BAPI reads: BAPI_MATERIAL_AVAILABILITY returns the batch‑segment ATP, which appears on the row where:
    • MRP element = BtsLoc
    • Stock segment = MIXTEST1 / FG (for the first batch)
    • Requested date = 02/13/2026

On that specific row, the Cumulative ATP = 880.

This is calculated as:

  • Total stock for batch MIXTEST1 = 973
  • Open requirements = 132 – 60 = 72
  • 973 – 72 = 880

That 880 is exactly what the BAPI returns in COM_QTY.

  1. Why you see 972

The 972 you’re seeing in your screenshot is coming from a different row, usually:

  • the plant‑level ATP
  • or the storage‑location‑level ATP
  • or a row that aggregates multiple batches

Those rows do not represent the batch‑specific ATP that the BAPI uses.

  1. How to verify

Scroll to the row where:

  • MRP element = BtsLoc
  • Stock segment = MIXTEST1 / FG

On that row, the Cumulative ATP column will show 880, not 972. 

Apologies for little, longer response but a longer explanation needed here to understand the flow. Feel free if you need more information.

entry.title: Two-Tier ERP with SAP Cloud ERP - 2602 Release Highlights
entry.content:
I am pleased to share the new addition from Two-Tier ERP as part of the 2602 release for  SAP Cloud ERP, and I would like to walk you through a couple of scenarios from this release. We're delivering effective ways to enhance strategic flexibility, streamline complex processes, and build an agile enterprise for you.

Let's dive into the highlights,

The Strategic Foundation: Mastering Your Data in a Two-Tier ERP World 

Many organizations operate in a Two-Tier ERP landscape, with headquarters running on SAP Cloud ERP Private  (SAP S/4HANA Cloud, private edition). and agile subsidiaries operating on  SAP Cloud ERP, i.e., SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition.

In a product-centric two-tier ERP setup, master data objects such as customer and product master data become critical prerequisites for both tiers. This is where SAP Master Data Governance provides a strategic foundation.

 

By running SAP MDG at headquarters, organizations establish a single source of truth where all master data is created, validated, and approved before being distributed to target systems.

This approach follows a simple but crucial principle:

Create and govern master data centrally. Consume it locally.

Subsidiaries running on SAP Cloud ERP receive governed, high-quality master data, ensuring clean, compliant operations. This not only strengthens group-level reporting but also significantly accelerates the onboarding of new acquisitions. As your organization grows, your master data remains a stable and trusted asset.

For a deeper exploration of this topic, I recommend the blog post “SAP Master Data Governance in a Two-Tier ERPLandscape with HQ & Subsidiary Approach” by Abhijit Bolakhe.

 
Advanced Warehousing Meets Cloud Agility

Another flexible architectural approach we support is integrating a decentralized SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) system with SAP Cloud ERP. This hybrid model allows you to maintain a clean-core cloud ERP while leveraging the rich, mature, and highly configurable functionalities of a full-scale EWM for your complex warehouse operations.

 Let's look at a typical process. For inbound goods, a purchase order in SAP Cloud ERP creates an inbound delivery that is sent to your decentralized EWM. EWM then orchestrates the entire physical process from unloading to put-away. Once complete, confirmations flow back to the cloud ERP, perfectly updating stock and financials.

Similarly, for outbound, a sales order triggers the process, with EWM managing everything from picking to loading, and finally sending the goods issue confirmation back to SAP S/4HANA Cloud. This integration offers a flexible yet perfectly balanced approach, combining advanced warehouse power with cloud simplicity.
The 2602 release for SAP Cloud ERP continues our commitment to providing a flexible, intelligent ERP. For wider types of industry, these updates deliver the strategic control, process efficiency, and commercial agility needed to thrive.
Please make it a point to deep dive into these EWM integration scenarios. We have two blog posts by Sahil Grover  The SAP Extended Warehouse Management Integration with SAP Cloud ERP - Part 1 and The SAP Extended Warehouse Management Integration with SAP Cloud ERP - Part 2

I hope this overview has been insightful.

Thank you for reading.

entry.title: Inquiry: Recommended procedure for manual margin adjustments
entry.content:
Dear Support,

We would like to request your guidance regarding the recommended procedure in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Professional Services) for performing manual adjustments to margin calculations within professional services projects.

Currently, the system automatically calculates the margin based on configured costs and prices. However, in certain operational scenarios, we need to apply corrections or manual adjustments to the reported margin due to:

  • Commercial adjustments agreed upon with the client.
  • Internal cost regularizations.
  • Corrections for discrepancies between planned and actual costs.
  • Management reporting requirements that necessitate reflecting a specific margin.

We would appreciate your orientation on the following points:

  1. Is there a standard functionality in SAP Cloud that allows for manual modification or adjustment of the margin within a project or its corresponding WBS element?
  2. If a direct adjustment is not available, what is the SAP-recommended procedure to reflect these adjustments (e.g., through manual cost postings, journal entries, pricing adjustments, or extensions)?
  3. Is it necessary to activate any additional functionality, BAdI, or specific configuration to enable these types of adjustments?
  4. What would be the impact of these adjustments on standard margin reports (such as Project Margin, Project Profitability, etc.)?

We appreciate your support in advance and look forward to your recommendations to ensure we proceed according to SAP Best Practices.

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Inquiry: Recommended procedure for manual margin adju...
entry.content:
Adjustments are handled through the Event-Based Revenue Recognition (EBRR) framework or manual postings.

  • App: Event-Based Revenue Recognition - Projects (F2129): This is the primary tool for margin management. It allows you to adjust recognized revenue and cost of sales without changing the underlying billing or time postings.

  • Manual Accruals/Deferrals: You can use this app to manually "revalue" the project. For example, if you need to reflect a specific margin for management reporting, you can manually enter a recognized revenue amount that results in your desired margin.

entry.title: Re: Joule - Document Grounding with SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition (RAG)
entry.content:
Comprehensive thank you.
The only challenge I had was creating the key. The format was not correct and Bruno giving me CERT errors.

Created this bash script to automate the extraction of Certs and Key, hopefully of use to someone else.

#!/bin/bash

if [ "$#" -ne 3 ]; then
echo "Usage: $0 <json_file> <cert_output_file> <key_output_file>"
exit 1
fi

JSON_FILE="$1"
CERT_FILE="$2"
KEY_FILE="$3"

if [ ! -f "$JSON_FILE" ]; then
echo "Error: File '$JSON_FILE' not found."
exit 1
fi

if ! command -v jq &> /dev/null; then
echo "Error: 'jq' is required but not installed."
exit 1
fi

jq -r '.certificate' "$JSON_FILE" > "$CERT_FILE"
if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
echo "Error: Failed to extract certificate."
exit 1
fi

jq -r '.key' "$JSON_FILE" > "$KEY_FILE"
if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
echo "Error: Failed to extract key."
rm -f "$CERT_FILE"
exit 1
fi

echo "Certificate written to: $CERT_FILE"
echo "Key written to: $KEY_FILE"

entry.title: Re: SAP SAC how to populate a measure value to all line i...

entry.title: Re: Best way to move data of contract header to sales ord...
entry.content:
First check Copy Control (VTAA) - this is the standard approach.

Maintain a Header Data Transfer Routine (VOFM) an assign it at header level VTAA. This is the cleanest and recommended solution for moving contract header data to sales order header.

Use MV45AFZZ (USEREXIT_MOVE_FIELD_TO_VBAK) only if the logic cannot be handled via copy control.

Standard first, user exit last.

entry.title: Company Code Carve out or Separation in SAP ERP system (SAP ECC & S4HANA On-Prem,Cloud environments)
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog outlines the typical business triggers that drive the need for Company Code Carve out in a productive ERP environment (ECC or S4HANA systems). It also highlights the possible realization methodologies to support such business transformations effectively.

Company Code Carve Out

A Company Code Carve-Out refers to the selective separation of data from a productive SAP instance into a separate target system. This data separation is driven by the organizational unit “Company Code” (technical domain: BUKRS) within the SAP system.

As part of the process, both higher-level organizational elements (e.g., Controlling Area, Operating Concern etc.) and lower-level hierarchical elements (e.g., Plant, Sales Organization, etc.) within the SAP enterprise structure are identified and analyzed to enable the separation of the entire legal entity’s business application data.

The dataset in scope typically includes all relevant data stored in SAP database tables and fields across all layers Viz. Customizing, Master, and Transactional data. This also encompasses customer-specific developments stored in Z or Y namespace tables.

Goal of Company Code Carve out is to give the divested business entity into an independent environment while minimizing the impact on the source system that contains the remaining data related to the relevant company codes.

Typical business use cases that require Company Code Carve out 

  1. Divesture of a Business Unit: It happens when the organization or group decides to sell off/dispose or close a part of the organization or subsidiary in order to raise cash/address regulatory or performance issues. With this activity, divested group can either become a stand-alone entity or independent company or strategic asset for a buyer.

  2. Country or Region-specific Separations/Decentralization of Central System: When an organization decides to spin-off or separates the country or region from their existing ECC or S4HANA landscape. These situations are primarily driven by the differences in the corporate laws, securities, regulations, tax rules and controls related to that country jurisdiction. Sometimes, this may also be driven due to the sanctions amended on specific countries leading to damage to the business in whole.

  3. Joint Venture: When organizations or partners decide to enter into JV with specific scope & duration to access country specific local markets, shared investments, risk diversifications and later decides to divest or spin off into a separate legal entity.

  4. Sale of Subsidiaries: An event occurs when parent organization plans to sale/transfer controls of a legal entity to another organization based on the board approvals, share holders’ resolution etc., for additional liquidity or strategic alignment on existing business.

  5. Strategic Refocus and Legal Restructuring: This kind of activity defines the business and reshapes the legal entity structure to support the strategy that organization thinks to refocusing their business. It helps in reallocation of capital to high advantage business or withdraw from misaligned businesses or non-core portfolio.

  6. Merger & Acquisitions: Mergers & Acquisitions are the transactions where organizations either combine or takes control over other to achieve strategic objectives/growth perspective. It provides opportunities to accelerate growth, portfolio diversification or consolidation.

All the aforementioned use cases require the complete historical transactional data of the respective legal entity to ensure uninterrupted business operations and to meet statutory and compliance reporting obligations.

Seamless operation and continuity of connected business applications are critical success factors for any divested or separated entity. Ensuring the continuity of open processes is essential for maintaining reporting dependencies and enabling informed strategic decision-making.

Furthermore, statutory reporting and audit trails of business application data represent critical data assets for any organization. They are fundamental to meeting country-specific data residency requirements and safeguarding the organization against potential legal and compliance risks arising from non-adherence to regulatory obligations.

To perform the separation of business entities/units (Company Code) from an existing SAP ERP landscape (ECC or S4HANA), SAP-DMLT service supports with SAP Company Code Carve Out solution that is tailored to address the aforementioned use cases with a cleaner strategy and data set that can let the organizations function independently and enables stable operations, financial reporting, auditing with the legal obligations.

Typically, objects in scope would be Company Codes and its relevant upper and lower hierarchical organizational elements (such as Controlling Area, Plant, Sales Org, associated master data – Customers, Vendors & Materials etc.)

Technical approach of Company Code Carve out

1. Company Code Clone & Delete

In this approach, the entire source system should be copied technically (clone) including repository objects, customizations, users and the business application data. Later, based on the user provided/confirmed company codes will be retained and all other company codes along with relevant org. units will be deleted from the cloned environment using SAP DMLT toolset.

This will technically delete the out-of-scope Company Codes along with its associated & shared data from target/cloned system, enables faster, cleaner and minimum disruption to business.

Business Outcomes (Value)

  1. Faster, Cleaner approach for Company Code Carve-out
  2. Reduced risk due to functional completeness
  3. Standard SAP tool to perform the carve out activity
  4. Limited testing required compared to general green field approach of only open balances
  5. Business Process Continuity & Faster realization
  6. Data history for statutory reporting needs

Transformation Scenario Limitations

  1. Suitable for smaller database
  2. Any additional data transformation expectation can't be embedded together

2. Company Code Transfer/Migration

As the name suggests, in this approach we will transfer / migrate only the relevant Company Codes with associated org. units along with the respective data sets only to the target empty system. It avoids the need for full copy of the source system compared to earlier explained 'Clone & Delete' approach.

P.S: Shell creation is the process of building a new & empty SAP system with complete repository, cross client objects and client specific customizing of source system without master & transaction data

Business Outcomes (Value)

  1. Minimal data footprint
  2. Lower infrastructure costs/repurpose any existing landscape
  3. Better control on the data set & objects (selection of relevant application) to be moved to target system/environment
  4. Possible to accommodate qualified transformations in any data harmonization (such as Rename of org objects, master data)
  5. Best suited for scenarios with a limited number of company codes being divested or for any country-specific carve outs - where the data set to be separated represents only a small or minimal portion of the overall database volume

Transformation Scenario Limitations

  1. Would require a longer project duration compared to clone-and-delete approach, as each application must be handled individually and might require additional, specific transformation capabilities embedded in the transfer logic.
  2. Relatively higher effort investment on validations and reconciliations

Realization Methodology of Company Code Carve-Out

Once a company code is productive or live with transactional data, any changes become restrictive. Moving configuration changes without transforming the already posted documents can lead to data inconsistencies in the live system, potentially causing critical disruptions in open business processes.

To separate any Company Code(s) from the live system, both the configuration, master and it’s associated transactional data must be handled consistently. For deletion of existing posted documents or transforming open documents, the SAP DMLT Company Code Carve Out Service is required. For more information, refer to the Company Code Carve Out Solution Brief.

Such data transformations in a live production system demand a highly precise and controlled changeover, ensuring consistency at the database level and seamless continuity of SAP software operations. Therefore, every relevant business scenario should be carefully reviewed in the system, considering current configurations and customizations across all connected application modules.

SAP DMLT offers a technical feasibility study to outline system data dependencies and prerequisite conditions. This is followed by a detailed customer workshop with the obtained statistics from the system. The realization of the transformation requirement is delivered under a separate professional consulting contract and executed as a project.

The overall delivery process is broadly divided into two parts:

1. Pre-Qualification of Requirement

It is essential to pre-qualify the business requirement to ensure the right-fit transformation solution is offered. This starts with gathering preliminary details about the business requirement and application usage within the system through a technical questionnaire, available at Mergers and Acquisitions, Divestments, Restructuring.

Based on the received responses, SAP can propose a feasibility study to assess the system, evaluate overall complexity, and deliver a detailed proposal. This assessment typically takes around two weeks, including the deployment of analysis tools in the system.

An SAP Solution Architect/Expert will perform the system assessment and present the key insights during a customer workshop session. This is followed by the provision of commercial details through the designated local SAP Market Unit account representatives.

2. Project Delivery

Depending on the complexity of the engagement, the required number of mock execution cycles will be determined during the scoping phase. In general, data transformation projects of this nature involve a minimum of 2-3 mock cycles before the production data cutover. The purpose of a mock execution is to simulate the complete production data carve-out/deletion, validate the results, and estimate the overall downtime required for the production system.

Each mock execution cycle includes several phases, which can be broadly classified as follows:

2.1 Technical Preparation Phase of Add-On (Uptime):

These types of transformation capabilities are deployed into the customer system as a separate add-on, delivered as a transportable content. The mock execution system should be a recent copy of the production system, with identical or closely matching hardware configuration that of the production environment. Once the tool content is deployed, the technical preparation phase begins, during which SAP DMLT experts prepare the transformation tool. This phase typically takes 2–3 weeks and includes uptime activities and the generation of technical programs for the relevant table-fields.

2.2 Data Carve-Out Phase (Downtime):

After the uptime preparatory activities are completed, the agreed business downtime begins. During this period, Company Code Carve-Out is performed directly at the database table-field level. All relevant Company Codes are separated along with the relevant Org Units in the respective application areas (e.g., FI-GL, FI-AA, ML, CO etc).

2.3 Post-Processing Testing Phase (Uptime):

Once the data carve-out phase is completed, the transformed system is handed over to the customer’s IT/application team for a sanity check during downtime. Upon successful completion of the sanity check, the transformed system is reopened for normal business operations.

Engagement with relevant SAP team or SAP Experts

If your business requirement involves any transformation related to Company Code aspects in a live, productive SAP ECC or S/4HANA system, you can engage with the SAP Data Management and Landscape Transformation (DMLT) consulting team through following ways;

  1. Create a Support Incident
    Submit an incident via the SAP ONE Support Launchpad under the component: CA-LT-SRV (Landscape Transformation Services), Or
  2. Contact via Email
    Reach out directly to the DMLT Global Consulting Experts Email: sap_dmlt_gce@sap.com

The SAP DMLT team will guide you through the pre-assessment process, feasibility evaluation, and project-based service engagement tailored to your specific system and transformation needs.

Reference SAP OSS Notes:

481938 - System Landscape Optimization Services

1462004 - SAP LT: Transformation Solution 'Company Carve-out'

Disclaimer: This blog is intended only for knowledge sharing about Company Code Carve-out/Delete service on SAP ERP (ECC or S/4 HANA) system which might help customer in their landscape transformation requirements. It should not be considered as guideline for performing such transformation services on their own in any of the SAP landscape. Any data transformation efforts should be thoroughly assessed in consultation with qualified subject matter experts, supported by a comprehensive system study, and aligned with the appropriate approvals from your auditors and relevant stakeholders.

entry.title: SAP Concur Payroll Integration via ICS
entry.content:
The Payroll Integration allows clients to transfer payroll data to their SAP Payroll system as a method for reimbursing their employees for approved business expenses.

SAP Concur ICS (Integration with Concur Solutions) enables seamless, automated data flow between SAP Concur Expense and SAP ERP systems like SAP S/4HANA or SAP ECC.

ICS supports payroll integration by enabling expense reimbursement data to flow directly from Concur to SAP payroll systems.

Payroll Integration helps to reduce manual work by automatically transferring expense financial posting data to the SAP Payroll system.

Once an expense report is successfully posted in SAP S/4HANA or SAP ECC system, it will automatically send the report to SAP Payroll system for processing, allowing companies to reimburse out of pocket expenses and accurately assign wage types and tax related details. ‑of‑pocket expenses and accurately assign wage types and tax‑related details.

A payroll document is created only after its related expense report is fully posted, ensuring accuracy and consistency in payroll and financial data.

Systems Involved:

  • SAP Concur – where employees create and submit expense reports
  • SAP FI – validates and posts the expense (no payment yet)
  • SAP HCM / EC Payroll – stores expense data as HR master data
  • SAP Payroll– calculates tax and pays the employee via payroll

System Landscape Options:

Payroll integration via ICS works with:

  • SAP FI + SAP HCM in the same system
  • SAP FI and HCM in separate systems
  • SAP FI in cloud + HCM on‑premise
  • EC Payroll (SuccessFactors)

1. Configure Payroll Integration in SAP Concur

Step 1: Login to Concur and navigate to Administration → Expense → Group Configuration

Select the respective group and Tick (✓) Enable Financial Integration setting and then Tick (✓) Enable Payroll Integration setting.

Once these settings are enabled, concur admin can be able to see the “Payroll Integration” setting under Company → Tools → Payroll Integration

Step 2: Under “Payroll Settings” select the expense group for which payroll Integration is activated. Tick (✓) the check box to use the payroll integration to reimburse employees belong to this group. Once this setting is enabled, all expenses will be paid through payroll.

Enter the below clearing External wage type codes that are maintained in HR/HCM system

  • Cash Advance Clearing Wage Type: This code is used to deduct the cash advances from the employee’s payable amount.
  • Personal Expense Clearing Wage Type: This code is used to deduct the employee’s pay by the amount they spent for personal use on the company card.
  • Payroll Clearing Account Code: This code is used to clear the payroll liability after the amount has been paid to the employee.
  • Benefits in Kind Wage Type: This code is used to record amounts that are treated as a taxable benefit for the employee.

Fill in the Default Wage Types fields. These wage types will be applied when no expense‑specific wage type has been defined on the Wage Types tab.

Once all the required details are entered, click on “Save” button to save the payroll settings.

Step 3: Navigate to “Wage Types” tab and map the wage type codes to the expenses maintained in SAP HR/HCM. This overwrites the default wage type mapping maintained in the Payroll Settings “Default Wage Types” settings.

Once all the wage types are mapped to the expenses, click on “Save” button to save the wage type mappings.

Note: If “Enable Payroll Reimbursement” checkbox is enabled then all the expenses will be transferred to SAP Payroll even if “Transfer Amounts to Payroll?” flag is not selected in the “Wage Types” tab.

To use Payroll for reporting only, then deactivate "Enable Payroll Reimbursement" setting under “Payroll Settings” tab.

If this setting is disabled, then we need to select the expense types that should be transferred to payroll only for reporting/taxation.

Navigate to “Wage Types” tab and select the respective expense types that should be sent only for reporting/taxation. Tick (✓) "Transfer Amounts to Payroll" Check Box for selected expense types. Click on “Save” to save the Wage Types settings. These expenses will be reimbursed via Accounts Payable but not via Payroll.

Note: Taxable travel allowances always flow to payroll.

Step 4: Configure Payroll Wizard in SAP Concur ICS

  • Login to SAP ECC or S/4 HANA system and execute the T-Code: “CTE_SETUP”
  • Open “Integration Activities” and enable “Payroll” connection
  • Configure “Payroll” ICS wizard with required mappings according to client requirements for payroll posting

 

2. Expense Report Payroll Process

  High Level Architecture Diagram:

Process Flow: SAP Concur → SAP ECC or SAP S/4 HANA (FI & Payroll Postings) → SAP Concur → SAP HCM → Payroll Run → Employee paid via payroll

Step 2.1: SAP Concur – Expense Report Process (Top Blue Box from above process diagram)

  • Employee creates and submits an expense report in SAP Concur (E.g., Breakfast, Taxi, hotel)
  • Review & fully approve the expense report by respective approvers & Processors.
  • SAP Concur sends all the fully approved expense reports which are in payment status “Processing Payment” to SAP ECC or SAP S/4 HANA (This is mandatory, even for payroll reimbursement)

Step 2.2: SAP Concur Expense and Payroll Posting in SAP ECC or SAP S/4 HANA via ICS (Yellow Box from above process diagram)

  • SAP ECC or SAP S/4 HANA automatically imports Expense reports into ICS wizard under Financial Postings → Concur Documents based on the scheduled import job.
  • SAP ICS validates the imported reports and verifies the associated cost objects, G/L accounts, and company codes.
  • Once all the expense data is processed without any errors, then expense documents are posted to SAP ECC or SAP S/4 HANA (on Payroll Clearing Account) successfully.
  • For Out-of-Pocket‑of‑Pocket expenses, SAP ECC or SAP S/4HANA doesn’t issue a direct payment to the employee or vendor. Instead, it posts the amount to a Payroll Clearing GL account which is maintained in “Payroll Settings” in SAP Concur, which allows the payroll team to reimburse the employee through their regular salary process.
  • SAP ICS sends success or failure status back to Concur
    • If Expense posting is failed → process ends here and employee/processor needs to correct and resubmit expense report in Concur
    • If Expense Posting is successful → Payroll process will continue

Important rule: No FI posting = No Payroll Posting

  • Once the FI posting is successful, SAP ECC or SAP S/4 HANA automatically imports successfully posted expense reports into Payroll ICS wizard for payroll posting based on the scheduled import job.

Step 2.3: Transfer of expense data to HCM via Concur

  • Once FI and Payroll posting is successful, Concur automatically triggers the HCM and sends the expense data from Concur to SAP HCM
  • Below details are transferred to HCM:
  • Employee ID
  • Amount
  • Expense types
  • Mapped wage types

Step 2.4: SAP HCM Master Data Setup for Concur Payroll Postings (Green Box from above process diagram)

 Import Reports

  • SAP HCM receives payroll-relevant expense data

Create Info Type 0579

  • Data is written into Info Type 0579
  • Info Type 0579 = External Wage Type Components

Each expense line item becomes:

  • A wage type
  • With an amount
  • Assigned to the employee
  • Stored temporarily for payroll

Key point:  Info Type 0579 is a staging area, but no employee payment yet 

Step 2.5:  Payroll Process

 Execute Payroll

Payroll program run for the payroll period and calculates the amount to be reimbursed for the employee after the necessary deductions. Employees can be able to see net reimbursement and deductions in their salary pay slip.

Note: Payment is made via Standard payroll bank transfer

Step 2.6: Concur Audit Trail update

Once the payroll posting is successful, then the status of the report will be automatically updated in Audit trail in Concur.

To view the Audit trail, select the respective report and go to → Details → Audit Trail to track the status in the expense report. 

3. Monitor Concur Payroll Posting Document Failures

Login to Concur and navigate to Company→ Tools → Payroll Integration → Posting failures tab. Under this tab, concur admin can be able to see all the failure posting documents.

Concur Admins can search for failed postings using the Report ID, Report Name, Employee ID, First Name, or Last Name.

By selecting a checkbox next to an expense report, admin can retry the payroll transfer by clicking on “Retry Payroll Transfer button” at the bottom of the screen.

4. Monitor Concur Payroll Document status

Under “Document Status” tab, admin can be able to search and check the status of the expense reports that are in “Success/Acknowledged/Ready”

5. General FAQ’s

Q1: What types of expenses are sent to Payroll?

A: Only out-of-pocket (cash) expenses—those paid personally by the employee—are sent to payroll for reimbursement. Corporate card transactions typically do not post through payroll unless any expense types selected for tax/reporting purpose. ‑of‑pocket (cash) expenses—those paid personally by the employee—are sent to payroll for reimbursement. Corporate card transactions typically do not

Q2: How are personal expenses handled by Payroll?

A: Any personal transactions charged to a company paid card are recorded under the Personal Expense Clearing wage type and deducted from the employee’s total payroll reimbursement. 

Q3: What are the key limitations associated with Payroll Integration?

A:

1) Partial employee payments in FI and HR are not supported and require customer‑specific enhancements, such as implementing a BADI in ECC or SAP S/4HANA On‑Premise.

2) Cash advance cannot be supported through Payroll Integration, as the feature does not support paying or posting cash advances

3) Payroll Integration will not support payments for IBCP, CBCP, or other company‑paid card transactions, since these are not intended to flow through payroll.

entry.title: Close Offboarding Processes automatically using Integration Center
entry.content:
Introduction

In this blog post, we will walk through how to automatically close offboarding processes in SAP SuccessFactors using Integration Center. Specifically, we will configure a scheduled integration that updates processes based on the employee’s termination date. By the end of this guide, you will understand how to set up the integration, map the required fields, and ensure the process status is correctly updated.

This configuration is useful for us, in the sense that often required tasks are not all completed on the platform and therefore the job responsible for closing the processes does not work, requiring manual cancellation.

1. Choose the Integration Type

The first step is to choose the integration type. In this case, we will use the Scheduled Trigger type and select SuccessFactors as both the source and destination system.

2. Select the Start Entity

For the start entity, search for ONB2Process.

3. Map the Process ID and the default values

In my case, I set the default values for Process Status and Task Status to "Completed". However, this configuration also works with other status values, depending on your requirement.

Important note: The operation must be set to Update/Merge.

Next, we need to map the Process ID. To do this, click on "Set as associated field" and search for Process ID from Process.

4. Define the Logic

In our scenario, we needed to close offboarding processes when the termination date was 30 days in the past, so we used the target date from process that corresponds to termination date :

5. Schedule and Test

After completing the configuration, you only need to schedule the job and test it. 🙂

The expected result is:

  • Process Status: Completed

  • Task Status: Closed

 

Optional: Cancelling Instead of Completing

If instead of completing the process you want to cancel it, you must also map the Offboarding Cancellation Reason field:

Conclusion

In this post, we demonstrated how to configure a scheduled integration in SAP SuccessFactors to automatically complete or cancel offboarding processes. By selecting the correct integration type, mapping the required fields, and applying appropriate date logic, you can fully automate offboarding closure when the required tasks have not been completed. This approach helps ensure data accuracy, reduces manual effort, and keeps your offboarding workflows aligned with business requirements.

entry.title: Re: Sユーザが削除できない
entry.content:
弊社バックエンドにて調査したところ、S-user * は「Partner users」ロールを保有しております。 このロールを他のS-userに移行しない限り、お客様ご自身で該当S-userを削除することはできません。

先ほど弊社にて該当S-user * を削除いたしましたが、 今後の利用上の不便を防ぐため、可能な限り「Partner users」ロールを他のS-userに移行されることをお勧めいたします。

なお、このロール移行作業は「Partner Support Team」のみが実施可能ですので、 以下のKBAをご参照の上、同チームまでご連絡いただけますようお願い申し上げます。

KBA 1271517 - Important Contact descriptions and how to view/change S-users with important functions - SAP for Me

関連する内容を抜粋致します、ご確認するようにお願い申し上げます。

To add/remove partner roles and authorizations for S-user IDs, the company's Partner Security Manager (S-user) must make the changes in the SAP Partner Portal according to KBA 2609123. Or, contact partner support using the partner Get Help page or contact SAP Partner Support using the "Get Support" on the partner "Get assistance" dashboard.

Tip: If you're not sure who your Partner Security Manager (PRM_SMGR) is or you need to update this user, you must contact partner support using the "Get Support" on the partner "Get assistance" dashboard.

entry.title: Automating Probation Period Extension Using Intelligent Services Center in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Automatic Probation Extension using Intelligent Service Center

Intelligent Service Center (ISC) is a tool provided by SAP in Employee Central that helps recognize events such as hiring, recruiting, time-off requests, and changes in job information like location or job title and etc. There is a standard set of events available in ISC. In this blog, we’ll walk through a requirement that helped automate a manual HR task with the help of ISC & IC.

Business Requirement:
If an employee who is under probation applies for sick leave, their probation period must be extended by the number of leave days taken.

Why this is needed:
Currently, HR has to manually check whether the employee who submitted the leave request is still under probation. If the employee is under probation, HR then needs to manually calculate the new probation end date based on the number of sick leave days taken.

There are chances that HR may overlook whether the employee is under probation, forget to update the probation period, or make errors while calculating the extension date.

To avoid this manual effort and reduce the risk of errors, ISC along with IC has been used to automate this entire process.

Solution Overview:

Our solution is a combination of:

  • Configurations in Time off object
  • ISC – preferred because this change takes place immediately instead of us waiting for the job to run.
  • IC – to change the extended date in job information

Configuration in Time off Object:

A field was created to store the extended probation end date.

This field gets populated during the creation of leave request.

This field ensured the logic is applied to only probation users.

Event Detection using ISC:

ISC by standard can detect Time off request.

In ISC we have configured only to detect the time off request in case a probation user applies sick leave.

So basically 2 condition:

  • Checks if the user is under probation
  • Checks if the leave type selected is Sick leave

Triggering Integration for Probation Update

After all conditions were met:

ISC triggered an Integration Center job

The following data was passed from Time off object to Job Information:

  • User ID
  • Event date (as absence start date)
  • Event reason (set the value as: Probation Extension)
  • Updated probation end date

Important Note:

Make sure to add save rule in Time off object to invoke the ISC - trigger worker absence event.

Configuration Flow:

Sick Leave Applied → Save Rule Calculates Extension → Leave Approved → ISC Event Triggered → Integration Updates Probation End Date

Conclusion:

As a result, HR users no longer need to manually track employees who apply for leave or update probation extension details themselves. By combining ISC, IC, and business rules, different levels of automation can be achieved in SuccessFactors. This not only reduces manual effort but also helps streamline HR processes and improve efficiency going forward.

entry.title: Re: Hi everyone, please help with the solution as per bel...
